sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Organophosphates are a common cause of poisoning within the developing world and result in a cholinergic crisis. Organophosphates can be found in readily available preparations such as insecticides, surface and room sprays, head lice shampoo, fumigation and crop protection

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- SLUDGEM: salivation, lacrimation, urination, defaecation, emesis, miosis
- DUMBBELS: diarrhoea, urination, miosis, bronchorrhea, bradycardia, emesis, lacrimation, salivation
- Agitation, seizure, coma

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Responder safety is paramount. Do not enter any area that risks the safety of responders
- Use appropriate PPE and remove the patient from ongoing contamination before treatment where possible
- Investigating why/how the toxicity occurred is important to rule out self-harm
- Respiratory failure from bronchorrhoea, bronchospasm, and muscle weakness is an early cause of death
- Atropine is first-line treatment for clinically significant cholinergic toxicity and should be titrated to drying of secretions and improved oxygenation rather than to heart rate alone
- Symptom onset may be delayed after some exposures, so ongoing observation is important

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance if applicable
- Position patient appropriately
- Administer oxygen if indicated
- Remove contaminated clothing and wash exposed skin with soap and water where appropriate
- Record vital signs and monitor for wheeze, bronchorrhoea, bradycardia, fasciculation, and reduced ventilation
- Have a low threshold for calling the on-call doctor or Poisons Information

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway
- Provide assisted ventilation if required
- Gain IV access
- Apply cardiac monitoring and 12 lead ECG
- Consider atropine in consultation with the on-call doctor; titrate to drying of secretions and improved oxygenation — not to heart rate. Repeat doses may be required and large total doses are expected in severe poisoning
- Consider fluids if indicated
- Consider benzodiazepines for seizures or severe agitation in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Oxime therapy, if used, should be guided by the on-call doctor or toxicology service

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Always offer engagement with Mind Matters or EAP if applicable
